title: Fixing Incorrect Type Inference for a Generic Function

statement.markdown: Given a generic function `createArray<T>()` that returns an array of type `T`, fix the implementation to correctly infer the type of the returned array based on the input type `T`. The function should return an empty array if `T` is not provided.
statement.notes: The function should be generic and support any type `T`. The returned array should have the same type as the input type `T`.

constraints.description: The number of elements in the input type `T`

examples.explanation: An array of type `number` with 3 elements

hints.text: Use the `T` type parameter to infer the type of the returned array

editorial.title: Editorial: Fixing Incorrect Type Inference
editorial.intuition: The key to solving this problem is to correctly infer the type of the returned array based on the input type `T`.
editorial.bruteForce: One possible brute force approach is to use the `any` type and then use type guards to narrow the type of the returned array.
editorial.betterSolution: A better solution would be to use the `T` type parameter to infer the type of the returned array directly.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution is to use the `T` type parameter to infer the type of the returned array and use the `Array<T>` type to represent the returned array.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The proof of correctness can be shown by demonstrating that the returned array has the correct type based on the input type `T`.
editorial.dryRun: Example dry run: `createArray<string>()` should return `[ ]` and `createArray<number>()` should return `[ 1, 2, 3 ]`
commonMistakes: Using the `any` type instead of the `T` type parameter
Not using the `Array<T>` type to represent the returned array
